data.title: EPR Properties: AI And Streaming Fears Overblown While Diversifying Away From Theaters
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered EPR Properties (EPR), I highlighted their robust AFFO growth, solid fundamentals, and well-covered, attractive monthly dividend as they are undergoing a significant portfolio transformation.

Following a solid year, ramped-up portfolio transformation, and a more attractive valuation and yield, EPR is upgraded to Strong Buy despite potential for near-term macro pressure, trading at a significant discount to its fair value.

Portfolio Transformation Ramping Up

EPR reported a solid 2025, with Q4 FFO being in line with the market's estimates alongside a beat on revenue, with the AFFO growing by ~7% to $398.2 million (slightly above my estimate of $395.80 million), while the AFFO/share also grew by a very solid ~6% compared to 2024.

The year came with significant portfolio rebalancing activities, expanding their golf portfolio alongside other experiential properties in Q4 and announcing a massive $168.3 million in dispositions in 2025, selling 33 theaters over the past 5 years and currently having 1 more vacant theater left, aiming at reducing this theater exposure further alongside their small education portfolio.

The company continued this expansion beyond Q4, recently announcing a deal for a portfolio of 7 regional parks in a $342 million transaction from Six Flags Entertainment (FUN), marking their largest acquisition since 2017 and further expanding their portfolio away from theaters - with the acquisition of 6 parks already closed and 1 more expected to close in Q2.

For 2026, they expect this to accelerate, with guidance for $400 million to $500 million in investment spending (as previously expected) and $25 million to $75 million in disposition proceeds throughout the year, while the FFO, as adjusted per share is expected to reach $5.28 to $5.48, meaning an increase of 5.08% YoY.

Financially, based on EPR's latest report, we continue to see a relatively normal position for a REIT, with debt that's covered by their real estate properties well, with their net debt to annualized adjusted EBITDAre standing at 4.9x as of the end of the year, below the lower end of their target range.

Meanwhile, although they have $1 billion undrawn on their revolving credit facility and all debt is fixed through interest rate swaps at a 4.4% weighted average interest rate, the current environment may push them into more expensive refinancing, which can add more pressure in the form of interest expenses. The company's CFO also highlighted the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

In November, we closed on $550 million of new 5-year senior unsecured notes at a coupon of 4.75%. And at year-end, we had $90.6 million of cash on hand and no balance drawn on our $1 billion revolver. Additionally, in December, we finalized our new ATM program. While no equity issuance is required to fund our plan for 2026, given that we project to be below the midpoint of our target leverage range at year-end without any such issuance.

Following their crash and the 5.1% increase in their monthly dividend, EPR now stands at a yield of about 7.3% at the moment of writing this, representing a very solid payout ratio of about 72.37% based on their 2025 AFFO, or even better if the AFFO grows.

I'll also mention the company's 3 preferred stocks since some of them are worth considering, in my opinion:

First of all, the redeemable perpetual preferred EPR.PR.G (5.75%, $25 liquidation preference) is a solid option, as despite being callable since November 2022, it should be safe for now given the fact that the company's common stock actually comes at a higher yield than the ~7.15% they're currently trading at, and it certainly looks more attractive now given the price of $20.14 it's trading at. Given the small base yield, I don't see this as something they'd call any time soon, as the cost of capital (debt and equity) is likely higher than this 5.75%, and even if they decide to call it for some reason, we're still talking about a ~24% profit, which is not bad at all.

There's also EPR.PR.E (with a 9% base) and EPR.PR.C (5.75% base), both of which being convertible perpetual preferreds issued back during the boom and subsequent bust back in 2006 and 2008, highlighting what issuing in a good and terrible environment looks like. They currently pay a 7.32% and 6.24% yield, respectively, and E continues to be the most attractive one now, in my opinion. That's because we are talking about an instrument that can't be forced into conversion unless the price appreciates 150% based on the applicable conversion price from back then, meaning about $77.40 at December 31, 2025 for Series E, which would imply prices similar to the levels from back before 2020, although I continue to believe that the common is better for my risk-reward appetite.

EPR should release their next earnings report on April 29th, with the market expecting FFO of $1.24 per share on 179.33 million in revenue. Given the ongoing portfolio moves and transition away from movie theaters, I expect them to continue this portfolio transformation for now, although I wouldn't be surprised to see an overall negative tone regarding the broader environment due to the ongoing Iran conflict.

Macro Uncertainty Rising

Right now, it's no surprise to see the stock's weak performance during March, as the Iran conflict can have significant implications on the economy and discretionary budgets, which would directly affect the company's tenants.

Not only are we talking about significant jumps in pump prices and potential for inflation in virtually everything that can last for longer than the market may initially believe due to the damage done by Iran's retaliatory attacks on their neighbors' energy infrastructure, but this inflationary shock can affect the Fed's next moves.

We can see treasury yields already jumping as a result of the market's initial response being a flight back to the dollar, and we already know very well what a higher-for-longer rate environment or even hikes can do to the valuation of most REITs, as these companies depend on the cost of external capital since they have to pay most of what they make back to shareholders.

With Trump's recent threat that "a whole civilization will die tonight," while others consider that the markets are "completely wrong" regarding the Iran conflict and believe that oil could even reach $200/bbl, it's hard to see a de-escalation happen overnight, although I still believe (and hope) that we're likely to see an end to this relatively soon given the economic, political, religious, military, etc. implications and casualties of a prolonged war.

However, that's not to say that a de-escalation would solve this overnight. In fact, we're very likely getting to a point where even a complete de-escalation today would still lead to major inflationary pressure in the near future, pushing central banks into rethinking their trajectory of rate reductions as they brace for the impact, with rising uncertainty surrounding the Fed's next moves while the ECB chief already stated that they're ready to hike as soon as possible.

EPR's Future?

Over the past years, we've had worries surrounding the movie theater industry's future due to the ongoing "streaming wars" and even AI disruption risk that fellow analyst Jussi Askola mentioned in his recent article, potentially leading to a change in the content production/distribution landscape.

Although this is a real risk, and finding another usage for a movie theater is very tough since we're talking about a very specific and specialized segment of real estate, it can still take several years (more like decades) until this would hurt the existing properties, in my opinion, as we're still talking about going to the movies to have an experience rather than just watching content. People had access to these movies well before AI or streaming services became popular, yet we still went to the cinema.

On the other hand, AI can boost the industry and result in more content available in cinema, which can be a counterargument to the expected impact. Note that I'm not talking about random AI content, but rather professionals using it to save significant time or cut budgets for otherwise expensive stunts, directive/script issues, etc., which can even be bullish for EPR's properties.

Yes, these are risks that come on top of the macro pressures that can fundamentally hurt REITs, but until/if we see them affecting the market (which can initially respond through less demand for new properties before being hurt), we're talking about properties that still generate solid amounts of money and can still be sold in the foreseeable future while EPR is advancing its diversification and ramping up their investments.

Regardless, it's very unlikely to see these hurt the company significantly by the time they achieve their diversification goals, which EPR's CEO also highlighted, stating that they plan to reduce this exposure to even less than 20% over the next 3 to 5 years, replying with the following when asked if this would be achieved through selling or adding new properties during Citi's Miami Global Property CEO Conference from March 2026:

I mean we've sold 33 theaters over the last year -- over the last 1.5 years [AUTHOR'S CORRECTION: this is a transcript/audio mistake; it's actually 5 years]. So we've done -- we're doing both, and we'll continue to look and do both. So again, it's not -- if there's an opportunity to sell and accelerate that, we'll take a look at that. So it's really about pulling both levers.

Risks Reiterated

Although the company might be mostly "out" of the theater industry by the time these specific risks for the industry would affect them, selling properties during such an environment can be terrible due to the Iran conflict and its inflationary pressure causing significant uncertainty and potential rate hikes or a higher-for-longer rate environment, at the very least.

Meanwhile, given their relatively elevated level of debt that's due over the next half a decade and will have to be refinanced year after year, it wouldn't be impossible to see higher interest expenses as a result, while issuing stock is also very expensive and hard to beat through regular real estate investments.

Valuation

For EPR's DCF to Equity, starting from their 2025 performance and the guidance, we should likely see an AFFO of about $418.11 million in 2026, which I'll assume can grow at a 4% CAGR (slightly below the market's estimates) despite their significant investments and potential for lower refinancing rates in the future, with a relatively conservative 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a 10% discount rate to take into account their volatility and specific risks, with 80.685 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $5.94 billion, for an intrinsic value of $73.67 per share, which continues to be well above the current $52.05.

Conclusion

EPR is upgraded to Strong Buy, trading at an attractive valuation and sustainable yield while standing to benefit from their portfolio transition and potential macro improvements in the longer term.

There are risks to consider, and there's room for this to go down more if the environment deteriorates and the market has to price in more pressure, but that's going to be the case for most REITs and stocks out there.

data.title: Caterpillar: AI-Driven Power Demand And Strong Backlog Support Continued Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered Caterpillar (CAT) in December 2025, and the stock is up a good ~20% since then versus a slight decline in S&P 500. I expect this outperformance to continue moving forward. Caterpillar is poised to see good revenue growth given its strong backlog, dealer destocking ending and secular demand from AI-driven data center and power infrastructure work. In the medium term a potential cyclical recovery in construction and mining business should also support revenue growth. The revenue growth should also benefit from price increases and expanding services exposure.

On the margin front, the company should benefit from operating leverage on volume growth. Further, pricing discipline and contractual pricing escalators should help to offset cost headwinds.

While the stock is trading at a premium to its historical levels, the stronger earnings growth profile justify a premium. According to consensus expectations, Caterpillar is expected to deliver strong double-digit EPS growth (~20% annual EPS growth) for the next few years. I believe the stock still offers good upside potential from current levels. Hence, I rate it a buy.

Recent Financial Performance

CAT’s net sales were up 18% Y/Y in Q425 driven by higher sales volumes across all segments. The price realization was neutral in the quarter.

Segment-wise, the Construction Industries segment’s sales benefited from higher sales volume across North America. In the Resource Industries segment, sales growth was driven by higher sales volumes and favorable changes in dealer inventories. In the Power & Energy segment, sales benefited from higher price realization, increased volumes, and strong demand across oil and gas and power generation end markets.

The company’s adjusted operating margin declined by 270 bps Y/Y to 18.3%, largely due to $1.03 bn in higher manufacturing costs from tariffs. On a segment basis, adjusted operating margin declined in Construction Industries and Resource Industries, while the Power & Energy segments saw slight improvement.

Outlook

Looking forward, I am optimistic about the company’s revenue growth prospects. Caterpillar exited 2025 with a strong backlog of $51 bn, up 71% Y/Y and $11 bn sequentially. The strong backlog provides a good visibility for future revenue growth. The strong demand environment is also helping the company to pass through tariff-related and other cost inflation. The management expects 2% price realization in 2026. For long-term contracts, the company has used escalation clauses to protect against input cost volatility. So, the pricing should be a good growth driver.

The dealer inventory destocking, which was a headwind last year, should abate. Dealer inventory declined by $500 mn during 2025 as dealers chose to reduce their inventories due to tariff-related macro uncertainty. However, contrary to their perception, the demand has stayed strong. I am not expecting any further destocking and the dealers to return to normalized practices in 2026. Considering the strong demand environment, dealers can start restocking as well which should support revenue growth.

In the Construction Industries segment, the company is benefiting from data center buildouts. Further, non-residential construction is seeing good demand supported by reshoring and IIJA funding. If we look at the pipeline of data centre and reshoring megaprojects in the U.S., this theme is expected to continue driving growth for the next several years. Also, while the recent Iran war has raised some concerns about inflation and further rate cuts this year, I believe the long-term trend for the interest rate is still lower. So, residential and light commercial activity should also improve in the medium to long run as interest rates decline.

Further, the management noted that China is showing early signs of recovery in above 10-ton excavator category and the Europe is stabilizing. Through Caterpillar financial, the company is offering interest rate buydown to support small equipment buyers which helped it gain share in 2025, and these share gains should continue moving forward.

In the Resource Industries segment, rising commodity prices should help revenue growth further. Heavy construction, quarry and aggregate activity remains solid. Mining fleet utilization is also elevated and average fleet age remain high which should drive new equipment and replacement demand. In addition, the company’s autonomous hauling solutions is another secular growth driver. There were 827 autonomous medium and heavy duty trucks in operation at year end 2025, up from 690 at year end 2024. Recent agreements like mixed-fleet deployments in Brazil should further support growth.

In the Power & Energy segment, the company should beneﬁt from good demand for its power solutions driven by data centres buildouts and increasing AI adoption. The company is getting large scale orders including multi gigawatt prime power agreements. Further, the company is seeing strong demand for reciprocating engines for applications upto 10 MW and solar turbines. Management is increasing its capacity to meet this demand and its engine capacity is expected to double and gas turbine capacity should also increase through 2030.

Beyond equipment sales, management is targeting services revenue of $30 bn by 2030, up from $24 bn in 2025. The shift toward power applications with high run-time intensity supports long-term services and parts demand.

Overall, I am optimistic on CAT’s revenue growth prospects supported by strong backlog, dealer inventory restocking, strong demand in power generation, healthy commodity prices, improving rate environment and price realization and increasing services exposure. I am also optimistic about the company’s margin growth prospects. The margins should benefit from operating leverage as the volume outlook is positive.

Further, price increases should also help margins. Management expects approximately 2% price realization in 2026, which should help offset cost headwinds. Given strong demand in the company’s end markets, the company should be able to continue raising prices while maintaining its competitive position. This pricing discipline combined with operating leverage should help the company to offset cost headwinds.

The company is also incorporating inﬂationary cost pressures and price escalation clauses in its long term contracts which should protect against input cost volatility. Further, the company is also optimizing manufacturing footprint and supply chain, which should improve efficiency over time. Overall, the company’s margin outlook remain positive supported by operating leverage, pricing discipline, contractual protection mechanism and sourcing optimization initiatives.

Valuation

Caterpillar is currently trading at 31.62x FY26 consensus EPS of $22.88 and 25.99x FY27 consensus EPS of $27.75, which is a premium relative to its 5-year historical average P/E of 18.69x.

I believe this premium is justified given the company’s improved growth profile. Caterpillar is well-positioned to capitalize on rapid growth of AI data centres and related power infrastructure which is driving demand for prime power solutions. Prime power applications generates higher lifetime services revenue compared to traditional standby solutions, which should drive long term margin growth.

Further, with rates eventually turning lower in the medium term, residential and light commercial construction should recover. This, coupled with strong infrastructure spending and higher commodity prices (supporting mining capex and replacement cycle) bodes well for the company’s growth in the longer run.

One interesting thing is that companies with significant direct exposure to AI-driven power demand, such as GE Vernova (GEV), continue to trade at higher forward multiple, around 60x. Since investors are assigning premium valuations to power infrastructure companies benefiting from AI expansion, CAT’s 26x - 32x forward multiple appears reasonable.

There is also a potential for earnings upside. The company’s backlog increased 71% Y/Y, which should support revenue growth. However, consensus is modelling high single digit growth in the near term. Given the strong demand environment, pricing discipline, operating leverage and expanding services revenues, I believe Caterpillar can beat estimates and post better expected revenue and earnings growth in the coming quarters/years.

I believe the risk-reward is attractive given the strong growth prospects driven by secular prime power demand, cyclical recovery in mining, improving earnings quality, and strong backlog.

Risks

There are execution risks related to the company’s capacity expansion plans. Any delays in facility ramp-up or cost overruns could impact margins.

My thesis anticipates that declining interest rates should support a recovery in residential and light commercial construction. However, if interest rate cuts are delayed or smaller than expected, the recovery in construction activity may not happen and could impact volume growth.

Another risk is commodity price volatility. Any decline in commodity prices could lower capital spending, delay equipment replacement cycles and impact the company’s Resource Industries segment.

Takeaway

I believe Caterpillar has strong growth potential. The company should benefit from secular AI-driven power demand and cyclical recovery across construction and mining end markets, while the stock trades at a premium to historical averages, improving earnings quality, strong backlog, and services mix improvement justifies the premium.

data.title: U.S. And Iran Agree To Ceasefire
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

Chip fab: Intel (INTC) joins the Terafab project with SpaceX (SPACE) and Tesla (TSLA) to build chips to power AI and robotics.

No respite: The U.S. rejects Ford's (F) request seeking aluminum tariff relief after the Novelis fire caused supply bottlenecks.

Fire incident: A massive fire engulfs Kimberly-Clark's (KMB) 1.2M square foot paper goods warehouse in Southern California.

Pursuing peace

The U.S. announced a two-week ceasefire, suspending its attacks on Iran in exchange for Tehran reopening the Strait of Hormuz. The news sent U.S. stock index up about 3%, while Asian and European equities also rose. Oil prices (CL1:COM) (CO1:COM) plunged below $100/bbl as fears of prolonged supply disruption eased.

Major breakthrough: "I agree to suspend the bombing and attack of Iran for a period of two weeks," President Donald Trump posted on Truth Social, saying the ceasefire is subject to Iran agreeing to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. "We received a 10-point proposal from Iran and believe it is a workable basis on which to negotiate." Iran's 10-point plan includes demands for its continued control of the Strait of Hormuz, lifting of all sanctions, acceptance of its nuclear program and withdrawal of U.S. forces from the region. Pakistan, the main intermediary in the negotiations, invited the U.S. and Iran to Islamabad for further talks this Friday. To note, Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said the ceasefire included Lebanon, although Israel said it did not.

Reopening Hormuz: Iran and Oman are reportedly planning to charge fees from ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz during the ceasefire. Iran will use the fees it collects for reconstruction efforts, while Oman's plans for the funds remain unclear, Iran's semi-official Tasnim News Agency reported. "For two weeks, safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz will be possible via coordination with Iran's Armed Forces and with due consideration of technical limitations," Iran's Araghchi said. Meanwhile, Trump said the U.S. will help with the traffic buildup in the Strait of Hormuz. "Big money will be made. Iran can start the reconstruction process. We'll be loading up with supplies of all kinds, and just 'hangin' around' in order to make sure that everything goes well," he posted on Truth Social.

Supply outlook: While oil prices eased significantly, analysts say it will take time for supplies to normalize. "Further price direction will hinge on whether talks translate into a durable agreement and a sustained normalization of flows through the strait, with volatility likely to persist during negotiations later this week," said ING Economic and Financial Analysis. According to Kpler data, 172 Mbbl of crude and products are still on the water in the Gulf, spread across nearly 187 laden tankers. "While the ceasefire creates a window for transit, flows remain conditional and operationally constrained," Kpler said. Willie Walsh, director ​general of the International Air Transport Association, warned that jet fuel costs may stay elevated as "it will still take months to get back to where supply needs to be."

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

This Is A Most Compelling Buying Opportunity

What else is happening...

Apple's (AAPL) foldable iPhone on schedule despite hiccups.

AI startup Perplexity's revenue soars 50%, ARR hits $450M.

Jefferies (JEF) discloses $42.8M writedown from MFS collapse.

Moody's cuts outlook on Blue Owl (OWL) fund to negative.

GoPro (GPRO) to lay off 23% of staff as part of restructuring.

DOD eyes funding boost for Lockheed's (LMT) AIM-260 program.

Chicago Fed's Goolsbee warns of successive inflationary shocks.

China buys the most gold in a year as Iran war pressures prices.

Ethereum (ETH-USD) stablecoin supply hits record high of $180B.

Greece to ban social media for children under 15 from next year.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan +5.4%. Hong Kong +3.1%. China +2.7%. India +4%.
In Europe, at midday, London +2.9%. Paris +4.6%. Frankfurt +4.8%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow +2.5%. S&P +2.7%. Nasdaq +3.5%. Crude -16% to $94.88. Gold +2.6% to $4,808.60. Bitcoin +4.4% to $71,536.

data.title: Applied Industrial: Growth Is Not Enticing Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been over two years since the first time I covered Applied Industrial Technologies, Inc. (AIT), so I thought I’d go through its performance over the last year because the company recently released its full-year 2025 numbers, too.

The company has been chugging along as expected, but unfortunately, the growth and margin expansion are not good enough to warrant a buy right now.

Performance

Starting from the top, AIT’s revenues showed a decent uptrend over the last year. It may not have been particularly rapid growth, but it is better than a downtrend. Looking at the segments in more detail, AIT’s Service Center-Based distribution segment was slightly pressured due to softer industrial activity, with organic growth coming in around 3% in the latest quarter. It is a growth, but it is quite weak indeed. Regarding its other revenue segment, the Engineered Solutions, this segment saw growth of around 19%, but most of that was due to the acquisition impact. Orders were up around 10% in the segment, which tells me there is demand. So, most of the total growth did come from the acquisitions, which kept AIT’s top-line performance intact, but I would prefer to see some decent organic growth.

On to the company’s profitability and efficiency, we can see a mixed bag of results over 2025. Gross margins remained steady, but the EBIT and the bottom line are down slightly in the second half of 2025. The company saw higher LIFO expenses during the period, which brought down its profitability slightly, as well as softer December sales. Q2 ’26 GAAP EPS came in at $2.51, which was a beat of 2 cents.

Looking at other important metrics in efficiency and profitability, the management is doing a good job of utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder equity quite efficiently, thus creating value. Additionally, it appears that the company also has a decent moat and pricing power since its return on total cost is well above the minimum I’d like to see of 10%.

Now, let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. AIT’s cash position was around $406m, while having around $572m in long-term debt. I don’t think this is a bad position at all. The company is quite profitable and can easily cover the interest expenses on the debt outstanding. The company barely pays anything on that debt. The latest figure was around $942K, while the company made $123 million in operating income, giving AIT a very solid interest coverage ratio of around 130x. A formidable balance sheet that can easily take up even more debt to further the growth.

Upcoming Quarter

The management tightened FY26’s EPS and sales guidance to $10.45-$10.75 from $10.10-$10.85 and 5.5%-7% from 4%-7%, respectively. EBITDA margin will be essentially flat at 12.2%-12.4%. So, the company’s updated guidance was more or less in line with what the expectations were from analysts.

Overall, the company seems to have been doing relatively well over the last year. I don’t see much improvement, but the company’s performance isn’t going down either. Just chugging along as it had in the past. I would have liked to see more operational improvements.

Service Center—Core MRO as key growth

The management sees the service center segment as a steady, MRO-centric grower. This growth is going to come from price, share gains, and tuck-ins, according to the management. I read this as the service center performance will be steady, but nothing exciting. Something along the lines of low single-digit organic growth, and maybe a bit more, depending on what those bolt-on and tuck-in acquisitions bring later over time. These are usually smaller in nature but still quite accretive to the overall performance.

This segment is built around selling and maintenance/repair parts and services, like bearings, power transmission, and fluid power MRO. This business does seem more reliable since maintenance and repair can be a bit more predictable. The spending is recurring and tied to keeping equipment running, so I think this will be quite resilient in any macro environment.

For now, since volumes were flat, the management pointed to the growth coming primarily from price increases. This could help the company’s operational efficiency, but you can’t raise prices indefinitely. The management also said that they see decent demand in technical MRO and highlighted some internal sales initiatives that will be the key drivers for 2026.

So, I am comfortable with around 3-4% growth in the long run, and anything above that, I would consider a massive success.

Engineered Solutions

This segment will be a lot more exciting, but it will also come with a bit more risk. Analysts and the management alike see this segment as the primary growth driver, with particular highlights within the Automation and advanced technology segment. In Q2, automation orders were up around 20%, so this contributed significantly to the overall orders being up 10% y/y. Customers seem to be feeling the need to automate more of their plants going forward, and so that is very positive for AIT, since they are sitting in the perfect spot that is comprised of components and engineering support. With labor prices continuing to climb, many firms are now turning to robotics investments to protect their margins in the long run. Additionally, automation reduces injury risks and improves consistency. These two are very critical for regulated industries.

In my opinion, the company will see some decent growth from data centers, semiconductors, and other verticals. There is still so much demand from data center hyperscalers that, frankly, I think it would be impossible not to make money if your operations are involved in any way, shape, or form in data center infrastructure and AI. The hyperscalers will be spending over $700 billion in capex this year alone, and I don’t see how this is going to slow down next year unless these enterprises suddenly calculate that all the investments they already made are not worth it. I don’t see that happening in 2027, because all of these capex dollars won’t yield anything for at least a few years, and then maybe they will be like, “That's enough,” and begin to wind down the spending spree and cut off the free money.

Valuation

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model to see what the company could be worth with certain assumptions I have made. Below are those assumptions.

As I said before, I am confident that the company will see some low single-digit growth in the long run, so I went with 4% for the model. For margins, I think a steady increase over time is achievable too, but seeing how the company’s operations have been improving, 20-30 bps a year improvements seem reasonable. The rest I kept close to the company’s historical averages. For my discount rate, I went with around 8.3%, which is very close to the company’s WACC, according to a quick Google search. With these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And with these projections, we get AIT’s fair value to be around $220 a share, which means currently the company is a bit pricy.

Closing Comments

The company would need to do a lot better in terms of the top-line growth and margin improvements before I would consider buying the company at these prices. I will be paying attention to how the following couple of quarters come in and what the management is going to say about the demand overall regarding the company’s products. I think we will see some growth acceleration because of the massive demand surge in data centers and AI, but I would like to see how well the management is going to capitalize on it and the robotics sector.

data.title: Valaris: Pricing Has Reset, Earnings Haven't Yet
data.description:
Introduction

Valaris Limited (VAL), the offshore driller, appears to be in a transition year. After a phenomenal 144.5% rally over the last year, partly driven by the Transocean Ltd. (RIG) merger news, multiples currently look high for a cyclical name. However, the current valuation appears fair for a company in transition. For one thing, they might reflect the company’s contribution to the combined entity’s pro forma earnings base. More importantly, they reflect idle rigs, legacy contracts, and a temporary ‘whitespace’ period rather than the economics of recently signed contracts, as these new contracts are being signed at considerably higher day rates than the legacy contracts. The question is whether the market is evaluating the stock based on current trough earnings or the contract reality with higher utilization and day rates.

Offshore Economics and Why Valaris Matters to the Combined Company

Offshore development is driven by multi-year contracts and reserve replacement, not day-to-day oil volatility. As such, management’s comments that 70% of new deepwater spending sanctioned over the next three years break even at $50 per barrel mean Valaris does not even need oil at triple digits to be significantly profitable. These projects are concentrated in core deepwater basins such as Brazil and Guyana, where high-quality reservoirs and established infrastructure support durable offshore activity.

The merger details make clear that Valaris is not just being bought for scale. It adds a modern fleet, especially jackups and high-spec drillships, broadens geographic reach, and contributes to a combined backlog of about $10 billion. Within Valaris itself, drillships account for about $3.0 billion of backlog versus about $1.1 billion for jackups, and drillship average day rates are roughly $416k in 2026 and $433k in 2027, far above jackup levels. The combination of Valaris’ jackup stability and drillship earnings leverage gives Transocean both higher margins and cash flow stability, something neither company fully had on a standalone basis.

Contract Repricing Is Already Happening Before the Deal Closes

Fourth quarter results confirm that repricing is already visible across the Valaris fleet before the merger even closes, which is supported by the fleet status report. The Brazil contract with Shell for DS-8 starts in Q1 2027, lasts for about 800 days, and is valued at $300 million total, at a mid-$300k per day base rate, which rises to approximately $428k per day with additional services.

Meanwhile, the DS-7 off Angola received an extension for 325 days starting in October 2026, which is expected to earn about $125 million. The DS-9, also drilling off Angola, got a two-year extension starting July 2026 at current market rates. In total, the new awards and extensions are worth approximately $900 million, increasing the total backlog to $4.7 billion.

We can also see a steady and meaningful increase in drillship day rates, with the current disclosed average at around $416k and expected to rise to $433k in 2027. This may turn out to be a conservative estimate, given that the individual contracts are between $428k and $450k for the DS-8 and DS-4. In any case, these prices are meaningfully higher than the legacy rates, which are implied to be closer to $300k.

With higher day rates already locked in and multiple rigs idling or warm-stacked, it looks like pricing has already reset to mid-cycle. However, reported earnings remain suppressed due to idle capacity and downtime. All this reinforces the idea that the transition is already underway, and actual EBITDA realization remains dependent on utilization going into 2026 and 2027.

From Contracts to Earnings, EBITDA Still Lags the Cycle

Q4 adjusted EBITDA was $97M, down from $163M in the prior quarter. Importantly, the decline was driven by utilization rather than pricing, as idle rigs and fewer operating days weighed on results. With overall drillship utilization at around 45%, there is a structural lag between contract awards and earnings recognition.

The bridge from contract awards, renewals, and day rates to actual EBITDA is fairly mechanical. Idle rigs get back to work, resulting in an immediate increase in the number of operating days. Contracts roll over to the higher rates, and higher revenue at a relatively stable cost base starts flowing into EBITDA.

The following calculations are indicative, not factual. If we assume day rates to reset from around $300k to around $420k at the midpoint, the increase adds roughly $44 million of annual revenue per rig. If we take a flow-through of 60-70%, this means an increased EBITDA of $25 million to $30 million per rig. If four additional drillships get back on the job, the company would be looking at a $100 million to $120 million EBITDA uplift.

Taking an approximate baseline run rate of EBITDA of $350-400 million and adding utilization and repricing, we get to a $500-600 million mid-case and $650 with excellent execution and full normalization of utilization and pricing. The resulting $500–600 million range effectively represents a base case that aligns with management guidance of $485 million to $565 million. This suggests the market is already pricing in a partial normalization of utilization and day rates.

The guidance range, however, reflects only partial normalization. With drillship day rates already above $400k and utilization still around 45%, a full conversion of backlog into operating days would imply additional upside. In other words, the market appears to be pricing the base case but not a full normalization scenario yet. This creates a setup where valuation reflects expected earnings, but not necessarily the full extent of upside potential if utilization normalizes more fully.

What Transocean Is Buying

Transocean’s roughly $5.8 billion all-stock acquisition of Valaris is doing several things. A reasonable assumption would be that they’re not paying for current earnings but rather for the $4.7 billion total backlog, repriced contracts at higher rates, and the expected earnings for 2027 in particular, as 2026 can be considered a transition year.

It’s not a bad deal considering Valaris’ current market cap of $6.8 billion, and a good deal if we assume 2027 earnings are correct at a forward multiple of 14.12x. This multiple is reasonable but not clearly discounted. Due to the nature of the all-stock transaction, a crucial point is that existing and new Valaris shareholders will not receive cash but shares in Transocean, and they would therefore be buying into the new combined entity, which introduces its own risks and catalysts tied to integration, capital structure, and offshore cycle timing.

Risks & Catalysts

The primary risk is that idle rigs don’t get back to work as quickly and efficiently as expected. If the contract backlog doesn’t convert into actual operating days, the rigs still require maintenance while waiting for revenue to start flowing in. This would have a direct impact on EBITDA, and considering that the market is already pricing in 2027 earnings, any delays in execution could mean significant downside.

Other risks to consider are more macro in nature. The new contracts already reflect significantly higher pricing, and any slowdown in the offshore drilling industry would potentially cap future contract rates. Lower oil prices or reduced CapEx could slow contracting, showing up in future day rates and utilization if supply rises. The all-stock deal means that current Valaris holders are exposed to Transocean’s share price, which can fluctuate between now and the deal close.

There are no major new catalysts in terms of a rerating, and the key remaining driver is execution. What could push the stock of both companies higher is that the conversion of backlog into earnings turns out better than expected, making forward EBITDA more visible in reported results. Aside from that, the completion of the merger would remove structural uncertainty.

What to expect from Q1 2026

Last Q1, the company printed a surprise loss with EPS of -$0.53, significantly missing estimates of $1.24. This year, analysts are a lot more conservative, expecting EPS of -$0.03. However, this isn't really the number to watch. The metrics that matter are fleet utilization and day rates. Contract timing is key here, as newly awarded contracts take time to convert into operating days and earnings.

Utilization is likely to remain below normalized levels as some rigs remain idle. However, news of even one or two drillships being put back to work would be a big positive, especially in combination with the new, higher day rates for drillships. For the rest of the year, investors will focus on whether the $485–565M EBITDA outlook is maintained or revised. A weak quarter wouldn't invalidate the thesis, but it would mean that higher EBITDA will take longer to show up in earnings.

Furthermore, investors will want more clarity on the costs of bringing rigs back online, as well as the costs of remaining idle. Reactivation costs and downtime associated with bringing idle rigs back into service may weigh on near-term margins, creating a temporary drag even as utilization improves. Finally, the market will be closely looking at any updates and management commentary regarding the merger, as the stock is now increasingly trading as a combined entity.

Conclusion

Valaris is a major player in the offshore drilling industry, and its fundamentals remain strong with projects at roughly $50 per barrel breakeven and durable demand. Day rates are increasing, and the roughly $4.7 billion backlog supports earnings growth as more rigs are put back to work in 2026 and 2027. However, much of this strength is already priced in after the past year’s rally, and investors are now effectively looking at how their VAL exposure will translate into the performance of the combined entity with Transocean. With the base case largely reflected in the valuation and with potential execution upside, I rate Valaris a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Commodity Idea investment competition, which runs through May 31. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Canadian Natural: The Gold Standard Of Canadian Energy
data.description:
Introduction

Canadian Natural (CNQ) has been one of those great winners from the current situation in Iran, as it has appreciated by nearly 13% since my last buy rating and, in all, appreciated by nearly 60% since I first started covering the company in July 2025. With that in mind, I want to assess if the firm still has forward momentum, given the fact that many believe that the upside in the oil market has already been realized.

Current Dynamics

The most direct bullish narrative is the current conflict in the Middle East, which has completely changed the risk profile of global oil supplies, as the world was initially grappling with a surplus of oil. The market perception of safe energy evaporated overnight following the joint military actions in the region in late February, resulting in the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. These are major tailwinds for Canadian Natural as Brent rallied toward $120 a barrel, pushing for a structural revaluation of the company's stock and the broader Canadian oil sands industry, as China is seeking to mitigate its exposure to Middle Eastern supply shocks.

Since the war, the WCS-to-WTI differential has been narrowing thanks to the disruption of similar heavy grades from the Middle East that have forced worldwide refineries to bid up WCS prices. This essentially increases the realized prices for every barrel CNQ producers, amplifying the impact of the price rally.

This all comes at the same time as CNQ has been firing on all cylinders operationally, as highlighted by the latest earnings release in which management has stated that the company was operating at the highest level in its 35-year history. Annual production reached 1,571,000 BOE/d, representing a 15% annual increase. Q4 even saw an acceleration compared to that average, at around 1,659,000 BOE/d, driven by the Oil Sands Mining assets, where upgrader utilization has reached a staggering 105% in Q4. This is a critical indicator of operational maturity and maintenance excellence, as CNQ can maximize the output of synthetic crude oil that often trades at a premium to WTI, further enhancing the margin profile of the firm.

To capitalize on the high price trend, CNQ also amended its shareholder return policy by changing the net debt thresholds that determine the amount of FCF returned directly to shareholders. Initially, CNQ committed to return 100% of its FCF once net debt reached $10B, which CNQ already achieved back in 2023. The board subsequently raised the threshold for enhanced returns, effectively accelerating the pace of share buybacks, as the level is now below or equal to $13B CAD for a 100% towards shareholder returns. The current authorization that goes through 2026 and Q1 2027 is set for 182,396,564 common shares, or around 10% of the public float.

The heightened price environment, as well as the acquisition of the Tourmaline Oil assets, has changed the overall forecast for 2026. Production is set to reach between 1,615 and 1,665 MBOE/d, a 20K improvement over the initial midpoint. On the nat gas front, CNQ is set to produce 2,560 to 2,615 MMcf/d for the year while reducing the operating capital required to $5.99B CAD.

The Trans Mountain Expansion pipeline also reached full capacity in April this year, much earlier than the previous forecast, highlighting how the world is in demand for heavy crude. The system is now moving at its full capacity of 890K barrels per day, meaning that CNQ and peers now have unprecedented access to Asian markets at the perfect time, when a large supply side has been disrupted.

Valuation

Despite the rise in crude prices, CNQ is still relatively cheap on a P/E GAAP basis, as it currently stands at 12.62, while other peers like Suncor are at 18.61 and Cenovus at 16.89. CNQ also used to have a great FCF yield, which first attracted me to the stock, and though it has run up, the Canadian energy firm still has a fair yield of around 6%. The yield in USD is also seeing one of its lowest levels since 2022 at 3.66%. Furthermore, I did a DCF analysis as well as going through different scenarios to grasp where the firm is in terms of inherent valuation.

Based on my DCF, there could be an implied share price of $71.95, a nearly 50% upside to current prices, which would make the current rally still have a strong set of legs. On the scenario side, the probability-weighted fair value comes out to $44.49 USD, a slight 8% downside, though that would be bypassing the current peak in crude prices.

On the growth front, CNQ plans to drill 448 net wells during the year with a balanced mix of heavy crude, light crude, and liquids-rich natural gas. Management has also outlined a $15B+ roadmap for incremental oil sands capacity. This also encompasses the Pike 2 Greenfield project, which is expected to reach around $2.8B and is designed to deliver 70k BBL/d of bitumen production. It is set for approval by the end of 2026, while the Jackfish Brownfield expansion will be aimed at debottlenecking the three Jackfish phases to add 30k bbl/d of bitumen output over the next five years.

Risks

The current high oil prices are linked to the conflict in the Middle East and the disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. The risk is that as the situation normalizes, that premium could quickly erode and CNQ's valuation would contract, returning to normal levels. Moreover, CNQ's position is dependent on the spread between WTI and WCS; if there is a slowdown in Asian demand as Middle Eastern heavy crude comes back online, it would likely reduce that spread and compress margins for the firm.

Canadian Natural also operates mainly in Canada, which has more stringent energy rules than the US. The Canadian government is advancing regulations to cap upstream oil and gas emissions at 35% below 2019 levels by 2030. These sorts of regulations already pushed CNQ to defer its $8.25B Jackpine Mine expansion due to these economic burdens.

Conclusion

With its track record of sustained capital return to shareholders plus its updated framework to return all of its FCF to shareholders, as well as the best operational timeframe for the firm, I believe that Canadian Natural is a great play despite the rally in stock prices. It is undeniable that the company's stock will decline on the potential news that Iran and the US sign a peace deal, but the high realized prices for the company should help it to accelerate its debt reduction. I keep my Buy rating on Canadian Natural Resources.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Markets Pop On Peace Hopes
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Stock index futures jump as Trump suspends Iran attack for two weeks. (00:13) GoPro (GPRO) cutting workforce by 23% as part of restructuring. (01:19) Massive fire at Kimberly-Clark (KMB) California warehouse weighs on shares. (2:09)

This is an abridged transcript.

Stock index futures are higher before the bell as President Trump agreed to suspend the bombing of Iran for two weeks, subject to the opening of the Strait of Hormuz.

As of the time of this recording, S&P 500 futures (SPX) jumped 2.7%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) soared 3.4%, and Dow futures (INDU) advanced 2.4%.

Crude oil is down 15% to $95 and Brent crude is down 13% at $94.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 2.3% and the DAX is up 4.7%.

Trump said the U.S. has received a 10-point proposal from Iran, and it is “a workable basis on which to negotiate.”

According to a statement from Iran shared by President Trump on Truth Social, the Minister of Foreign Affairs said if attacks are halted they will cease their defensive operations. It goes on to say that safe passage through the Strait is possible if it’s coordinated with Iran’s Armed Forces.

GoPro (GPRO) is restructuring its business to lower operating costs and improve leverage. This includes a workforce reduction of about 145 employees, or roughly 23% of its staff.

The job cuts will take place in Q2 and are expected to be substantially completed by the end of this year.

As a result of severance payments and healthcare benefits, GoPro (GPRO) expects to incur a charge of $11.5M to $15M, including cash expenditures of ~$1.5M in Q2, ~$5.5M to $8M in Q3, and ~$4.5M to $5.5M in Q4.

The reorganization comes as the company struggles to earn a profit over the past two years, most recently reporting a loss of $0.02 per share on the lack of any meaningful revenue growth.

GoPro (GPRO) shares are down 48% so far this year, underperforming the S&P 500 by 44 percentage points.

A massive fire at a Kimberly-Clark (KBM) warehouse in Southern California put pressure on shares on Tuesday.

KBM closed Tuesday 4% lower. But there’s somewhat of a reversal happening so far in premarket action, KBM is up 2 percent.

According to the fire department, the flames grew “exponentially,” at the 1.2 million square foot facility, resulting in the roof collapsing and forcing firefighters to retreat from the fire.

It appears the fire may have been set intentionally by an employee of the warehouse. A person of interest is currently in custody.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Iran, Oman to charge fees for Hormuz transit during ceasefire - report

Shipowners eye escape for 800 vessels as Hormuz reopening looms

More departures at The Trade Desk as three execs depart -- report

Catalyst watch:

  • FedEx (FDX) will hold an investor event focused on the FedEx Freight business ahead of the spinoff planned for June 1. Key topics could include margin improvement, network efficiency, and the role of data and automation in future growth.

  • Zscaler (ZS) will participate in the Wells Fargo 2026 Software Symposium in Menlo Park.

  • Costco (COST) will issue its monthly sales report.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Levi Strauss (LEVI) +10% – Shares jumped after a strong Q1 beat, with consolidated revenue up 14% Y/Y and 9% on an organic basis, driven by broad-based growth across regions.

Economic calendar:

  • 10:30 am EIA Petroleum Status Report

  • 2:00 pm FOMC Minutes

data.title: T. Rowe Price: Outflows Are Increasingly Structural
data.description:
Thesis

T. Rowe Price Group, Inc. (TROW) saw accelerating institutional outflows in Q4 2025, due to which it missed earnings expectations. Assets under management (AUM) growth over the past 3 years has been purely market-driven due to continuous outflows. Effective fee rate (EFR) compression and my expectation of the retirement-based solutions offered by the target date franchise (TDF) to become a negative for net flows over the next decade combine for a flattish growth outlook. The company's strongest suit is its dividends, which are well covered and growing. However, the margin of safety is shrinking due to free cash flow growth stagnating. All of these factors lead me to give TROW a Hold rating, with a target price of $101.71.

About the Company

TROW is an asset management company that provides investment advisory services to both institutional and retail clients. The company's AUM stood at $1.80 trillion as of February 2026. TROW's AUM is primarily concentrated in equity, followed by multi-asset, fixed income, and alternatives. The firm is known for its active management approach, which has provided strong returns historically; however, the higher EFR commanded by this model is proving to be a headwind. TROW's TDF is among the industry's largest actively managed solutions and has partially helped reduce net outflows.

The company is considered a dividend aristocrat, having consistently raised its dividend for almost the past 40 years. The quarterly dividend stands at $1.30/share, up 2.4% from the previous year.

Net Outflows Are Becoming Increasingly Structural

TROW has seen cumulative outflows of $272 billion since 2021. The firm's AUM growth for the past 3 years has been driven only by the market appreciation of its investments, masking continuous outflows. This is especially true for equity investments, which are the primary source of net outflows. Given that equity strategies tend to carry relatively higher fee rates, there is a negative effect on both revenue and margins due to outflows in this category.

Before 2021, the organic growth rate (calculated as net flows divided by initial AUM) was consistently positive; however, this has changed recently. Be it bear markets, such as in 2022, which had a spillover effect on net outflows in 2023, or bull markets, such as the past 3 years, the organic growth rate has been persistently negative after 2021. This is the clearest evidence for flows structurally becoming negative.

Flows were previously linked to how well the market was performing and, consequently, how TROW's investments would fare. However, there is a weakening relationship between the market change of the firm's investments and the direction of net flows for the past 3 years.

2025 specifically was not a good year for the investment attractiveness of TROW's funds due to passively managed peers doing better, making only 28% of the firm's AUM outperform its passive peer median. The key driver for passive funds outperforming TROW was initial growth for the year being concentrated in large-cap tech names.

Recent relative underperformance, along with TROW's higher fee structure, potentially explains the net flows from institutional clients turning from +$9.8 billion in 2024 to -$30.4 billion in 2025, with institutional outflows accelerating meaningfully in the fourth quarter to -$17.3 billion. Currently, 5-year performance is the more relevant lens for institutional clients due to the contrast of TROW's equity AUM outperforming its passive peer median in the 5-year window (22%). This contrasts with the equity AUM performance for the 3-year period being 55%. The difference between these two periods captures the growth stock underperformance in 2022.

Institutional reallocations tend to be sticky due to the process of plan sponsors undergoing lengthy internal review and fiduciary documentation before reinstating a terminated mandate. The 2022 underperformance would be rolling off the 5-year investment performance in 2027, which may improve its perception to DC plan sponsors. However, any improvement relies on consistent outperformance through 2026-27. Even if outperformance is achieved, reprieve in the form of affecting institutional flows based on the 5-year performance would take significant time. This shows that even a medium-term improvement in performance may not be sufficient to reverse the outflows.

Going forward, without consistent outperformance against peers, TROW risks continued outflows in its equity segment, particularly from institutional clients, where fee sensitivity is high.

The Retirement Moat Is Under Threat

The TDF has, over the past years, supported net flows. This is often cited as TROW's moat in the form of continuous inflows through DC plans, in which the firm is selected as the target date solution provider. However, the TDF will eventually increase outflows as an increasing number of boomers reach their required minimum distribution (RMD) age of 73. The population curve of people reaching RMD age is currently turning, with live births per year accelerating from 3.6 million in 1950 to ~4.2 million in the mid-1950s to early 1960s, a difference of around 17%. The inflection point will come around 2027 to 2028, which corresponds to births in 1954 to 1955, which is when live births per year crossed the 4 million mark. In comparison, the live births per year for those entering the workforce are 4 to 4.1 million. There will also naturally be a major gap between the distributions received by people post-retirement and new inflows from those entering the workforce at entry-level salaries. This is evident by inflows into the TDF being relatively stable for the past few years, averaging $9.1 billion, and becoming $5.2 billion in 2025.

The TDF is a subsection of TROW's multi-asset component of AUM. This is important to note, considering that only 7% of the firm's AUM in its multi-asset segment outperformed its passive peer median in the 1-year timeframe, an abysmal performance. This could be the potential catalyst for net outflows from mandate losses in the TDF for Q4 2025 being $5.4 billion, without which inflows for the year would have been on pace to reach their usual ballpark.

Hence, despite TROW proactively trying to expand its TDF AUM by expanding internationally and expanding its market share in blended target date funds, which are also growing faster than passive target date funds, it would remain difficult for plan sponsors to choose TROW as their target date provider considering 2025's underperformance, which would roll into the longer timeframes. For perspective, the firm's multi-asset performance in the 5- and 10-year timeframes relative to the passive peer median was 95% each in Q4 2024 but declined to 53% and 77% for the 5- and 10-year timeframes, respectively, in Q4 2025. This indicates that a single year's underperformance has almost halved the 5-year timeframe's previously strong track record.

Hence the TDF, long considered TROW's most durable source of inflows, is concurrently facing pressure from demographic headwinds and performance deterioration.

Where TROW Gets Credit, and Why It Isn't Sufficient

TROW's performance in the fixed income segment has been strong, with 76-80% of fixed income AUM outperforming its peer median in all timeframes. Due to this, flows into the fixed income segment have been positive for the past 8 quarters consecutively. However, these inflows, totaling $25.1 billion for the past 2 years, are not nearly sufficient to counteract outflows from the equity segment, totaling $126.9 billion.

TROW is gaining market share and expanding its blend target date solutions, which are growing faster than passive solutions, and this is a strategic move made by the management of the firm. However, the fee structure of this model is lower than that of actively managed solutions, and an increasing proportion of these solutions in TROW's AUM will only further compress the EFR.

Following the acquisition of Oak Hill Advisors (OHA) in 2021, the firm's AUM included an increased offering of alternative assets, especially in private credit. The alternatives segment's contribution to TROW's AUM has also been expanding, growing from $48 billion in Q4 2023 to $59 billion in Q4 2025. The alternatives segment also commands an almost 50% higher fee rate, excluding performance fees, than all other segments: 56.7 basis points as compared to 38.4 basis points for all other segments on an aggregate basis. Growth in alternatives, which should continue due to the proposal to include them in 401(k) plans, is one tactic being used by management to stabilize margins and reduce the decreasing EFR for the firm. However, this segment is currently too small to meaningfully change the outlook for TROW, with it contributing to only 3.3% of the firm's total AUM. TROW also has $21.6 billion worth of unfunded commitments, which, if called up, would increase the proportion of AUM of alternatives to 4.4%, still not substantial enough to alter the outcome of existing challenges.

As mentioned earlier, 2022's performance rolling off on the 5-year timeframe represents a potential cyclical tailwind, though it is one reliant on outperformance from 2026 through 2027.

Despite the headwinds mentioned earlier, a dividend cut remains unlikely due to TROW being debt-free and having significant liquidity.

Dividend Sustainability

In 2025, TROW generated over $2 billion in free cash flow (FCF), which means that the FCF payout for dividends was 57%, and if we include buybacks, the FCF payout stood at 88%. I am assuming bull, bear, and base case market returns of 10%, 0%, and 5%, respectively, and the corresponding organic growth rates of -2%, -5%, and -3%. The EFR, including performance, for 2025 was 39.6 basis points, which I will assume decreases by 1 basis point each year due to the shift in investment vehicle mix and fee competitiveness.

The highest weight should be given to the base case, followed by the bear case, due to outflows and weakening margins. These figures show that despite bearish assumptions and assuming a growing dividend, TROW is well positioned for the dividend to be covered purely through the FCF generated. Beyond this, consider that TROW has a debt-free balance sheet and $3.4 billion in cash and cash equivalents.

If the dividend coverage percentage creeps higher, as shown in the bear case, management would be forced to stop share repurchases, which would reduce the boost given to EPS. Also note that in this scenario, dividend growth would be unlikely due to being constrained by lagging FCF growth from outflows and EFR compression.

Valuation

The market currently values TROW at a 1-year forward P/E of 9.08, which implies 2026 earnings of $2.16 billion, and assuming the same net profit margin as 2025, implied investment advisory fees for 2026 are $6.84 billion. Upon applying an EFR of 38.6 basis points, the implied average AUM for 2026 is $1.77 trillion, which is in line with my estimates of the most weightage being given to both the base and bear cases.

This indicates that the market has priced in the expectation for outflows to continue, with an organic growth rate between -3% and -5%. The 1-year forward dividend yield is 5.77%, and the base case expected FCF growth is 2.2%. This indicates that TROW is being valued as a low-growth, high-yield equity. It is plausible to assume a low FCF growth of 2.2%, since the challenges faced due to shifting demographics will increase over the next decade. Along with this, migration away from actively managed funds is expected to continue, considering mediocre returns and higher fee rates. A forward dividend yield of 5.77% is not sufficient to prove that TROW is undervalued due to the fundamental issues with the firm, and hence I expect this figure to continue being over at least 5%.

The cost of equity for TROW is 11.2%, considering a risk-free rate of 4.33%, a market return of 9%, and a beta of 1.47. Applying a growth rate of 2.2% on a base FCF of 2025, the fair market cap (FCF/(r-g)) of TROW comes out to $22.23 billion. The target price per share comes to $101.71, an upside of 13.9%, which does not cross my buy threshold of 15%. Hence, I would give TROW a Hold rating.

Though TROW trades at a low forward P/E multiple and a higher than usual dividend yield, this is potentially a value trap. The firm has structurally declining growth and unfavorable shifts in its mix, due to which TROW has a limited ability to consistently compound earnings.

Risks to the Thesis

Flow stabilization is a major risk to the presented thesis, which would mainly be driven by consistent outperformance against peers. 2022's performance rolling off in 2027 would show an improvement in performance in the 5-year timeframe. TROW's investment style has usually favored growth over value. A sustained market environment in which growth is favored over value would prove recent underperformance to be cyclical. Consequently, institutional inflows would increase, leading to an improvement in net flows.

Operating margins could improve from the expense control program employed by TROW's management and also market-driven AUM growth. This would invalidate arguments for flat to slightly contracting margins.

The alternatives segment could expand faster than anticipated due to the proposal for alternatives to be included in 401(k) plans. This would support the currently declining EFR due to the higher fee profile of these strategies.

A strong bull market could drive higher-than-expected market-driven AUM growth, increasing revenue and FCF growth while also improving operating margins.

Conclusion

TROW is fairly valued currently, considering outflows shifting to a structural nature, the expected decline in flow stability provided by the TDF, and declining EFR. There are some positives, such as the expansion into alternatives, strong dividend coverage from FCF generation, and the expansion into blended target date solutions. However, these are not significant enough to stem the structural issues central to TROW.

data.title: ClearBridge Dividend Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
By John Baldi & Michael Clarfeld, CFA

Diversification Rewarded Amid AI and Geopolitical Shocks

Market and Performance Overview

Vladimir Lenin famously said, “There are decades where nothing happens and weeks where decades happen.” With two radical developments unfolding in the last three months — the war in Iran and AI’s accelerating displacement of software engineers and the software industry — the first quarter was truly a quarter where it felt like decades happened. Despite the significant turmoil, the ClearBridge Dividend Strategy outperformed, remaining largely flat gross of fees while the S&P 500 Index declined 4.3%.

Tactically, we benefited from our significant underweight to information technology (IT, which declined 9.2% in the quarter) and our significant overweight to energy (which surged 38.2%). Strategically, we benefited from our commitment to broad diversification amid a market that has become massively concentrated.

Timing in markets can be a funny thing. Software developers have been using AI to accelerate coding for the last two years; yet, only in the last two months have investors realized that coders were inadvertently training their replacements. As users of Claude Code multiplied in the first quarter, the S&P 500 software sector declined 24% — a staggering amount for what appeared to be a fundamentally sound industry.

Whether AI spells the end of the software business is an open question. Some think AI will replace most applications, while others think it will threaten only marginal ones. Given the unprecedented dynamism and speed of AI, we do not pretend to have the answers. Instead, we focus our attention and investments in sectors that align more closely with our sphere of competence. We currently own just one software stock — Microsoft (MSFT) — and one stock — ADP (ADP) — with a small, and we believe well-defended, software exposure.

Interestingly, while AI has crushed investors sentiment toward software, it has yet to shake their steadfast conviction in the hardy outlook for the semiconductor industry. The iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), a widely quoted proxy for the semiconductor complex, rose 9% in the first quarter. The semiconductor industry group is now the largest in the S&P 500 at 15%. Superficially, this makes sense; demand for chips is insatiable and the outlook for data center construction is beyond robust. But the value of equities derives not from their outlook over the next two to three years, but the next 10 or 20.

"Volatile times also contain a silver lining, offering us the opportunity to take advantage of dislocations to high-grade the portfolio."

While AI unleashed volatility in the digital world, the war with Iran unleashed volatility in the physical world. Given our large investment in energy, the portfolio benefited from the resulting rise in oil prices. We have no idea how war will unfold. We have been pleasantly surprised by markets’ resilience thus far, but we anticipate escalating negative impacts if supply disruptions persist. The U.S. is relatively insulated from impacts of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz due to its domestic energy production. Other parts of the world, however, particularly Asia, will face increasing physical shortages and significant economic contractions unless shipping resumes soon.

While we maintain a large overweight to energy, we have taken advantage of rising stock prices to exit two of our holdings, EQT (EQT) and Enbridge (ENB). We have focused our energy investments in our highest-conviction ideas: Williams (WMB) and Exxon Mobil (XOM). EQT always represented more of a tactical investment in an improving U.S. natural gas market, rather than a long-term investment in a franchise energy company. We made substantial profits in EQT over a four-year holding period and have decided to move on.

After a long and profitable investment in Enbridge, we sold the position to concentrate our pipeline investments in Williams, which possesses a superior balance sheet and growth outlook. Further, with the U.S. pursuing confrontational trade policies toward Canada, the risks to Canadian companies dependent upon exports to the U.S. are elevated. Our investment in Williams is predicated on growing North American natural gas production and surging power demand from data centers. Rising oil prices have little direct impact on the company and its business should continue chugging along, regardless of the outcome with Iran.

Exxon Mobil, however, as the largest private oil producer in the world, directly benefits from the events in the Persian Gulf. Higher oil prices will drive bumper earnings and cash flows, but that is not the only thing Exxon has going for it. Exxon’s robust production growth from low-cost basins will propel volume increases and margin expansion through the end of the decade. We have modestly trimmed our position as the stock has soared, but we maintain a significant investment in the company.

Portfolio Positioning

In a choppy first quarter, we took advantage of market volatility to reduce some positions on strength and add others on weakness, upgrading our holdings and further diversifying the portfolio. We bought four “new” holdings in the quarter. Two of the four — Blackstone (BX) and Otis (OTIS) — we have owned before. While we sometimes sell great businesses because the valuations are extended or fundamentals deteriorate substantially, we never stop following great companies. Blackstone and Otis both sold off in the quarter, and we took advantage of those declines to welcome these old friends back into the portfolio.

Alongside our purchase of Blackstone, we significantly increased our exposure to Apollo Global Management, as concerns around private-credit markets improved risk-reward profiles for both. While losses in credit will inevitably rise from current low levels, both companies are well-positioned to navigate the cycle. Their long-duration capital enables them to weather the ups and downs, while their copious dry powder positions them to play offense. Alternative asset management remains a growth industry, and we believe we are acquiring these two best-in-class franchises at attractive prices.

As the leading elevator manufacturer, Otis is a best-in-class industrial company. While new construction activity ebbs and flows, Otis’s earnings are predominantly derived from its aftermarket repair and maintenance business, which is not economically sensitive and grows every year. While AI will disrupt many industries, well-situated industrial companies should prove resilient and accrete value over time.

Alongside Otis, we purchased shares of Honeywell (HON) during the quarter. Honeywell possesses formidable franchises in controls and aerospace. Its pending reorganization (it is splitting into three companies) should improve focus and execution while highlighting value.

We funded these purchases through monetizations of defense contractors and AI-related technology companies, both of which benefited from feverish investor enthusiasm during the period. As the geopolitical temperature has risen over the last 18 months, defense stocks have soared. Given our large holdings in defense, Dividend Strategy has benefited strongly over the last few years. At current prices, and given increasingly statist commentary from the federal government, we believe better opportunities exist elsewhere. We exited Northrop Grumman (NOC) and trimmed L3Harris (LHX) and RTX (RTX).

In IT, we exited Oracle (ORCL) and trimmed Broadcom (AVGO). Our five-year investment in Oracle proved highly profitable as the company transitioned its business model from licensing to software-as-a-service (SaaS). In the last year, Oracle went all-in on building data centers for AI customers, pushing its backlog north of $500 billion. In 2025, the stock surged as Oracle announced eye-popping AI contracts; we took advantage of that surge to begin exiting the position. More recently, investors have begun to question the return profile of these projects, given the hundreds of billions of dollars in required capital investment and their overdependence on one, single customer: OpenAI. Aligning with OpenAI seemed like a no-brainer two years ago when it was the clear leader in AI, but with Google’s Gemini and Anthropic’s Claude catching up to ChatGPT, Oracle’s concentrated bet on one player now seems questionable. We sold our remaining Oracle shares in the first quarter of 2026.

On the semiconductor side, we modestly reduced our position in Broadcom to fund our new investment in Taiwan Semiconductor (TSMC)(TSM). While Broadcom remains well positioned, and we remain constructive on the stock, the risk-reward outlook has diminished as the shares have tripled over the last two years. Further, whereas TSMC prospers regardless of who wins the semiconductor race (TSMC manufacturers chips for all the major semiconductor companies), one can conceive of scenarios where Broadcom could become less relevant in the future.

Outlook

While the U.S. is protected from the worst impacts of the energy disruption, higher prices are driving higher inflation. The longer these prices remain elevated, the more persistent and challenging the inflation and interest rate outlook will become. A slowing global economy combined with higher inflation and interest rates could present strong headwinds to markets in 2026. We continue to emphasize broad diversification and will exercise caution navigating the twin challenges of AI disruption and the war in Iran.

Our diverse portfolio emphasizes high-quality companies with low risk of disintermediation supported by strong financial characteristics and reasonable valuations. We anticipate continued dividend growth, driving increased cash returns and providing a meaningful offset to inflation. Volatile times also contain a silver lining, offering us the opportunity to take advantage of dislocations to high-grade the portfolio. We believe we are well-positioned for the current environment.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Dividend Strategy outperformed its S&P 500 Index benchmark during the first quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy saw positive contributions from five of 11 sectors: the energy, materials and industrials sectors were the main contributors, while the financials and IT sectors were the main detractors.

On a relative basis, outperformance was driven primarily by stock selection in the health care and communication services sectors, overweights to energy, materials, consumer staples and real estate, and underweights to IT and consumer discretionary. Stock selection in consumer staples, energy and real estate detracted.

On an individual stock basis, the main positive contributors to relative returns were Exxon Mobil, Williams Companies, Air Products and Chemicals (APD), Texas Instruments (TXN) and an underweight to Microsoft (MSFT). Apollo Global Management (APO), Capital One (COF), Automatic Data Processing, Unilever (UL) and not owning Chevron (CVX) were the main detractors.

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, we exited AvalonBay Communities (AVB) in real estate. We also received shares of Waters (WAT), a high-end lab equipment and consumables provider in the health care sector, following its spinoff from holding Becton, Dickinson (BDX); we are maintaining a stub position while we evaluate the new company’s dividend policy.

John Baldi, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Michael Clarfeld, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager
