data.title: Partners Group: You're Getting Paid 5% To Wait
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Partners Group (PGPHF) is not a classic dividend stock. But at a ~5% yield, it offers something unusual: you are getting paid while waiting for the private equity cycle to normalize. The market currently prices the company as a cyclical alternative asset manager. In my view, this misses the bigger picture. Assets under management (AUM) continue to grow, management fees provide a stable base, and exposure to infrastructure and private equity supports long-term demand. You are effectively getting paid to wait for a normalization of the cycle.

Company Overview

With approx. With $185 billion in AUM, Partners Group is one of the largest listed private market managers in Europe. That said, not all alternative asset managers are directly comparable. Business models differ across the peer group. Firms such as Apollo Global Management (APO) and Ares Management (ARES) have a strong focus on private credit, while others like EQT AB (EQBBF) or CVC Capital Partners (CVCCF) are more concentrated on traditional buyout strategies. More recently, private credit has been in focus due to gating measures at players such as Blue Owl Capital (OWL), which has weighed on sentiment across the sector. Partners Group sits somewhat in between, with a more diversified platform across private equity, infrastructure, and private debt.

The chart above puts this into perspective. While Partners Group is smaller than the largest US-listed players such as Blackstone (BX) or KKR & Co. (KKR), it operates at a comparable scale to leading European peers.

In my view, this is an important point. The company has a level of scale where it benefits from the key advantages of the business model: operating leverage, global sourcing capabilities, and access to institutional capital. From here, size is less of a differentiator. What matters more is growth, product mix, and capital allocation. As a result, I do not see scale as a structural disadvantage for Partners Group. If anything, the company should be viewed as a fully established global player rather than a regional niche manager.

The revenue mix is reasonably diversified, although it is important to keep in mind that the figures include performance fees, which can fluctuate significantly from year to year. Overall, revenues are generated from two main sources: recurring management fees and more volatile performance fees. Private equity clearly remains the dominant contributor, accounting for close to 60% of revenues.

Private credit has been in focus recently, but for Partners Group, the situation is more nuanced. Management highlighted that less than 2% of total AUM is exposed to credit-related software investments, which are at the center of current concerns. In my view, this indicates that some of the headline risk is overstated, even though the company is not fully insulated from a weaker environment.

Infrastructure has emerged as one of the strongest growth drivers within Partners Group's platform. As the chart shows, it has significantly outgrown the other segments over the past years due to strong demand.

Private equity still accounts for the majority of revenues, but the relative importance of infrastructure has increased over time. In my view, this is an important shift. Infrastructure is typically less volatile and more predictable than traditional buyout strategies. Returns may not be as high, but they tend to be more stable, which makes the asset class attractive for institutional investors. Given the ongoing need for investment in energy, transport, and digital infrastructure, this is likely to remain a key theme going forward and an increasingly important contributor to Partners Group's growth profile.

Revenue Model

The revenue model is fairly straightforward. Management fees provide the stable base and are primarily driven by AUM growth. Fee margins have remained broadly stable over time, which shows the scalability of the business. Performance fees, however, are more volatile and depend on exit activity and market conditions. This explains much of the variability in reported earnings.

In my view, this distinction is key. While headline revenues can fluctuate, the underlying growth in AuM and management fees remains the more relevant long-term driver.

News

What stands out to me is the stability of EBITDA margins. Despite the swings in revenues, margins have remained consistently high, which shows the asset-light nature of the business model. This is a key strength. The company has a flexible cost structure and can scale expenses up or down depending on market conditions.

This flexibility is primarily driven by personnel expenses, which represent the largest cost component and are closely linked to performance. A large part of compensation is tied to performance fees, so bonuses move with the cycle: higher in strong years and lower when activity slows.

The dividend track record is also very solid. Over the past 10 years, dividends have grown at a CAGR of around 13%, supported by the expansion of the business. That said, the payout ratio is consistently close to 100%, which in my view is too high for a traditional dividend stock. I view this as the result of the underlying earnings volatility: while management fees provide a stable base, performance fees can be lumpy and lead to elevated payout ratios in less profitable years.

Therefore, I would not classify Partners Group as a classic income stock. Instead, the dividend should be viewed as a function of the cycle rather than a fully stable income stream.

Valuation

The dividend is a central part of the investment case, but I think it is often misinterpreted.

At a yield of around 4.5-5%, Partners Group appears attractive from an income perspective, especially compared to peers. However, the consistently high payout ratio, often close to 100%, suggests that the dividend is not as stable as that of a typical income stock. In my view, this is not a weakness but rather a reflection of the business model. The company distributes a large portion of its earnings, which themselves are partly driven by performance fees and therefore inherently cyclical. As a result, the dividend should not be viewed as a fixed income-like stream but rather as a variable distribution that moves with the cycle.

I use a simplified DCF to estimate fair value, focusing on normalized growth rather than current earnings.

Over the past decade, revenue grew at around 14% per year. That said, this is inflated by performance fees and a very supportive environment for private equity. Going forward, I assume a more conservative 8% growth rate through 2030 and broadly stable margins.

This implies an AUM of approx. $300 billion by 2033 (based on a 1.7% revenue margin), well below management's long-term target of $450 billion. In my view, this provides a reasonable margin of safety. Based on these assumptions, I arrive at an intrinsic value of around $1,300 (CHF 1,080) per share, implying moderate upside from current levels. While the valuation is sensitive to assumptions, the current price already reflects a fairly cautious scenario in my view.

I use a CHF-based discount rate (in line with the reporting currency), which explains the low WACC. The outputs are calculated in CHF and translated into USD at roughly CHF 1 = USD 1.25.

Depending on the assumptions, the valuation range is fairly wide, but in my view the current price already reflects a cautious scenario.

H Model Assessment

As a sanity check, I also apply a simplified H model based on dividend growth. The output is broadly in line with the DCF valuation. That said, I would not put too much weight on this approach. Given the cyclical nature of earnings and the high payout ratio, dividend growth is unlikely to follow a smooth path. This limits the usefulness of this model.

Importantly, both my DCF and the dividend-based cross-check point to a similar valuation range, which increases my confidence in the result.

Peer Comparison

On a headline basis, Partners Group trades at around 17-18x earnings, below most global peers such as Blackstone or Ares Management. However, a simple P/E comparison can be misleading. Earnings in this industry are driven by performance fees and therefore fluctuate with the exit environment.

In the current setup, weaker deal activity is temporarily depressing earnings across the sector. At the same time, underlying drivers such as AUM growth and fee-related earnings remain intact. In my view, the current valuation reflects cyclical weakness rather than a structural issue. You're effectively buying a high-quality platform at a below-average multiple.

Risk to my thesis

There are some factors that could significantly change my investment thesis.

First, the private equity cycle could remain weak for longer than expected. If exit activity stays subdued, performance fees would remain under pressure and earnings recovery could be delayed.

Second, AUM growth could slow. While long-term demand for private markets remains intact, a weaker fundraising environment or lower allocation from institutional investors could impact the company's growth.

Third, the dividend could come under pressure. Given the high payout ratio and the cyclical nature of earnings, a prolonged downturn in performance fees could lead to lower distributions than currently expected.

Conclusion

In my view, Partners Group offers an interesting combination of a high-quality private markets platform and an above-average dividend yield. The market currently appears to focus on the cyclical slowdown in performance fees and the elevated payout ratio, which creates the impression of a riskier income profile. However, this overlooks the underlying strength of the business, like continued AuM growth, strong margins, and a scalable cost structure.

At current levels, investors are effectively being compensated with a ~5% yield while waiting for a normalization of the private equity cycle. I therefore view the current valuation as attractive for long-term investors who are willing to look through near-term volatility.
